Based on feedback, we’ll refine the alert interface and expand to a larger public beta through partnerships with banks or cybersecurity awareness programs. Support from the ideathon will help us with dataset access, mentorship, and credibility to collaborate with fintech and government safety initiatives.

column3: “Ambulances often struggle to reach hospitals on time during emergencies because of heavy traffic congestion and poorly maintained roads.”
column4: Ambulance drivers, emergency response teams, and patients in critical conditions.
column5: Studies show that every 10-minute delay in emergency response can reduce survival chances by up to 30%. Bengaluru alone reports dozens of avoidable deaths annually due to traffic-induced delays.
column7: 25+ short interviews with daily commuters and 3 ambulance drivers near the PES University and Rajajinagar area.
column8:
Many commuters mentioned frequently seeing ambulances stuck at busy junctions during peak hours.
Drivers said they often have to break traffic rules or jump signals to clear the way, creating chaos and risk.
Ambulance drivers shared that potholes and uneven roads force them to slow down even during emergencies.
Commuters expressed stress and confusion when an ambulance appears suddenly in dense traffic, as there’s often no clear way to move aside.
column9:
“People want to help, but there’s no system. Everyone just honks or jumps the signal, it’s dangerous for everyone.”
— College student, PES University
column10:
TAM (Total Addressable Market)
~15–20 million emergency ambulance trips occur annually in India.
Majority in urban areas where traffic congestion severely impacts response time.
column11: SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market) ~4–5 million ambulance trips yearly across major metros (Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi, Chennai). Average urban ambulance speed: 15–20 km/h, leading to 10–20 min delays per trip.
column12: SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market) Focus on Bengaluru – around 1–1.5 lakh trips/year. 70% experience significant delays due to traffic and poor road quality. Target impact area: 5,000–8,000 emergency cases/year within our campus and nearby zones.
column13: Average delay per trip: 10–20 minutes → 1.5 million + minutes lost annually in Bengaluru. Lives at risk: 25–30% of patients in critical cases; delays contribute to 200–300 preventable deaths/year. Economic impact: ~₹50–70 crore/year from fuel wastage, staff overtime, and delayed treatment.
column14:
Using regular heavy-traffic routes: Ambulances often use the usual main roads.
Taking shortcut or service roads: To avoid traffic, ambulances sometimes use shortcut or service roads.
Diverting to nearby small hospitals or clinics: In severe traffic situations, many choose to reach smaller hospitals for immediate help.
Manual traffic interventions: Occasionally, police manually clear intersections.
column15:
Using regular heavy-traffic routes: Ambulances often use the usual main roads, but during peak traffic hours, this results in long delays, wasting the patient’s crucial golden minutes.
Taking shortcut or service roads: To avoid traffic, ambulances sometimes use shortcut or service roads; however, these routes pass through residential or commercial areas, limiting speed and causing further delay.
Diverting to nearby small hospitals or clinics: In severe traffic situations, many choose to reach smaller hospitals for immediate help, but these facilities often lack the necessary equipment and expertise for critical emergencies.
Manual traffic interventions: Occasionally, police manually clear intersections, but this is inconsistent and depends on human coordination, delaying response time.
column16: Current traffic management systems lacks an integrated, real-time mechanism to detect and prioritize emergency vehicles, resulting in delays during critical situations.
column17: Timely (T): Urban traffic congestion has reached critical levels, causing unprecedented delays for emergency vehicles. Addressing this issue now is essential, as the growing city population and rising number of emergencies have intensified the risks associated with response delays.
column18: Important (I): The quantified impact includes thousands of emergency cases each year experiencing significant delays, leading to preventable loss of life and economic costs. Recent studies highlight that even a 10-minute delay in ambulance response can reduce survival rates by up to 30% in major metros.
column19: Profitable (P): The financial opportunity in Bengaluru alone spans a substantial recurring service market, addressing over 1–1.5 lakh ambulance trips annually. Reliable solutions targeting even 5,000 cases per year represent a considerable and sustainable revenue stream for innovative service providers.
column20: Solvable (S): Our team possesses the necessary skills in IoT, traffic systems, and operations management to develop and implement a practical solution. With hands-on knowledge and multidisciplinary expertise, we are equipped to bridge technology and real-world application effectively.
column21: Contextual (C): As students living and commuting in the impacted urban areas, we directly experience these challenges and have unique access to the affected community for piloting. This proximity enables us to gather real insights, test solutions rapidly, and ensure our approach aligns with genuine needs.
column22: The problem of delayed ambulance movement is urgent, significant, economically viable, technically achievable, and directly observed within our community, making it the right problem to solve now.
column23: We envision a city traffic system that recognizes and prioritizes ambulances automatically. When an ambulance is approaching an intersection during an emergency, the traffic signal adjusts on its own to clear the way — ensuring uninterrupted movement and faster medical response saving lives in critical situations.
column24:
Removes dependence on human reaction: Today, the ambulance relies on drivers noticing the siren — our system eliminates this uncertainty.
Faster response than manual police control: No need for control rooms or traffic police to intervene — the priority happens instantly.
Works even in noisy and crowded environments: Sirens can be ignored or unheard in traffic — but the actual traffic signal cannot be ignored or missed.
Does not require new infrastructure: It builds on existing traffic signal networks, making adoption realistic and cost-effective.

What is novel about your solution?
Smart cities already have digital traffic signals, and hospitals already have well-equipped emergency vehicles but there is no
direct link between them. Today, ambulances cannot influence a signal even when every second matters.
Our idea creates that missing bridge by enabling real-time priority communication between ambulances and traffic signals
something current traffic systems simply do not support.

Phase 4: City-Wide Expansion : Using the pilot's success data, we will work with Bengaluru Smart City to fund and roll out the  ___.                                                         system across the entire city, making it the new standard for emergency response.

column23: Our proposed solution is a mobile application that connects hostel or PG residents with security staff to streamline and secure parcel deliveries. Students can pre-log expected deliveries by entering details like name, room number, delivery type (e.g., Amazon, Flipkart, local courier), and estimated arrival time. This information is instantly shared with the security team, who verify and mark parcels as received when they arrive. The app then generates a unique OTP, known only to the student and security, which must be physically shared to collect the parcel—ensuring secure handover and preventing mis claims or theft.
column24: Our app guarantees secure, reliable parcel handover by directly connecting students and security staff, eliminating missed deliveries, misclaims, and manual coordination.
column25: From the Google Forms, we understood that many people have having problems due to lack of secured delivery pickup points and hence we  came up with a solution.  We propose a mobile application that bridges communication between hostel/PG residents and security staff to streamline parcel deliveries.  How it works:  Student logs delivery details like  Name  Room Number  Expected date and time  Delivery type( Amazon, Flipcart, Local courier elc)  Security receives notification  When the parcel arrives, they verify the details  Mark the parcel as “Received” in the app  App generates a secure OTP  Shared only between the student and security  The student must physically provide the OTP to collect the parcel  Why is there an OTP?: This system ensures secure handover, prevents parcel theft or misidentification, and eliminates the need for manual  coordination or unreliable workarounds. It’s simple, scalable, and tailored to hostel life

column15:
Pedestrians rely on guesswork — most people we spoke to said they “just cross when others cross,” even if vehicles are moving fast.
Traffic guards try to manage manually, often waving hands to stop vehicles during rush hours, especially near schools and hospitals.
Drivers depend only on road markings — no visual or audio cues when a person is about to cross.
Elderly and differently-abled people wait longer or avoid certain roads entirely due to fear of accidents.
column16:
There is currently no responsive and inclusive pedestrian safety system that reacts instantly to real-world movement and visibility conditions.
Our research showed that people want a crosswalk that “feels alive” — one that senses movement, warns vehicles, and adapts automatically without needing human supervision or complex tech to maintain.
column17: Advances in AI, haptics, and spatial audio make it possible today to build reliable navigation assistance for visually impaired individuals, and rising global focus on accessibility makes this the right moment to act
column18: Over 2.2 billion people worldwide face vision impairment, leading to reduced independence, slower mobility, daily navigation stress, and safety risks, making this a deeply impactful global challenge.
column19: Assistive-tech adoption is growing, government and NGO funding is available, and even capturing a small share of this global market creates a meaningful multi-crore opportunity through devices, subscriptions, and institutional partnerships.

column22: With rising accessibility needs and breakthrough AI-wearable technology, we are uniquely positioned to solve a high-impact mobility challenge for visually impaired individuals, creating a scalable and socially meaningful solution with strong adoption potential.
column23:
We hypothesize that combining AI-based vision, IoT sensors, and haptic + audio feedback can enable visually-impaired individuals to navigate complex environments safely and independently.
 By fusing real-time obstacle detection, context-aware decision logic, and smart crosswalk communication, the system can provide reliable, intuitive guidance even without visual input.
 If successfully implemented, this hybrid network of wearable and roadside modules will significantly reduce accidents, increase mobility confidence, and set a new assistive-technology standard.
column24:
All-in-one navigation safety — real-time obstacle + traffic awareness.
Reliable and inclusive — functions offline, day/night, even in noisy areas.
Affordable and scalable — modular design deployable in cities and campuses.
Empowers independence — restores confidence and mobility for the visually impaired.
column25: We plan to develop a functional prototype and conduct a small-scale pilot with visually-impaired users through accessibility support groups and institutions, gathering real-world feedback to refine and optimize the solution before wider deployment.
